title: The day-3 male newborn who stopped feeding
blurb: A previously well newborn who decompensates 48 hours after the first feed. The lab that distinguishes this disorder from its cousins is orotic acid in the urine.

patient.complaint: Stopped feeding overnight, now hypotonic and breathing fast.

opening: 3-day-old term boy, exclusively breastfed since hour 12. Mom called this morning because he refused two feeds, then became 'floppy.' On arrival he is lethargic with a GCS of 9, RR 64, and seizures on the monitor. Bedside glucose is normal. ABG shows respiratory alkalosis. Plasma ammonia comes back at 720 μmol/L. What do you want to ask, examine, or order next?
history: Term boy, uncomplicated pregnancy and delivery, Apgars 9 / 9. Fed within the first hour, latched well, mom voluminous milk. Mother had a much older brother who died of 'something at the hospital' as a baby. Mom is healthy, no exposures.
exam: HR 168, RR 64, T 36.4, SpO2 99%. Hypotonic with poor head control, anterior fontanelle full and tense. Periodic deep sighing breaths. Liver edge palpable 2 cm. Pupils mid-position reactive. No dysmorphism.

prompts.diagnosis: What is the most likely diagnosis?
prompts.mechanism: Briefly: why is the urine orotic acid elevated, and how does that distinguish this from CPS-I deficiency?

pinpoint.prompt: Click the urea-cycle enzyme that fails in OTC deficiency.
pinpoint.correctMsg: Yes — ornithine transcarbamylase. With OTC blocked, mitochondrial carbamoyl-P backs up into the cytosol and gets shunted into pyrimidine synthesis (CAD pathway) → orotic acid spills into the urine. Citrulline is LOW because it can't be made.
pinpoint.wrongMsg: Not quite. Look for the step where carbamoyl phosphate is joined with ornithine.
pinpoint.revealMsg: Target: OTC (Carbamoyl-P → Citrulline). Loss-of-function here gives the classic low-citrulline / high-orotic-acid / high-ammonia neonatal phenotype.

mcqs.stem: Why is OTC deficiency the only urea-cycle defect that classically presents with HIGH urine orotic acid?
choices: Carbamoyl-P accumulates in OTC deficiency and is shunted into the cytosolic pyrimidine pathway
OTC is required for orotate degradation
All urea-cycle defects raise orotic acid equally
mcqs.explanation: In OTC deficiency, mitochondrial carbamoyl-P (which CPS-I keeps making) can't proceed to citrulline and overflows into the cytosolic CAD pathway → carbamoyl-aspartate → dihydroorotate → orotate. CPS-I deficiency removes the source of carbamoyl-P, so orotate is NORMAL or low. That distinction is the classic exam point.

mcqs.stem: Why is plasma ammonia toxic AND the patient hyperventilating?
choices: Ammonia crosses the blood–brain barrier and is detoxified by astrocyte glutamine synthetase, which depletes α-KG and drives cerebral edema; brainstem chemoreceptors respond with hyperventilation → respiratory alkalosis
Hyperammonemia produces a high anion gap acidosis with respiratory compensation
mcqs.explanation: In astrocytes, ammonia + glutamate → glutamine (via glutamine synthetase). This is osmotically active and pulls α-KG out of the TCA cycle. The combination of osmotic edema and α-KG depletion creates the encephalopathy. Centrally-driven hyperventilation produces the classic neonatal respiratory ALKALOSIS — UCDs are NOT acidotic, distinguishing them from organic acidemias.

mcqs.stem: Initial management of this baby's hyperammonemia includes all EXCEPT:
choices: Protein-free IV dextrose + insulin to suppress catabolism
Sodium benzoate / sodium phenylacetate (or phenylbutyrate) for alternative N excretion
High-protein feeds to provide essential amino acids
mcqs.explanation: Stop protein, drive anabolism with glucose + insulin, use scavengers (Na-benzoate / phenylacetate) to bypass the broken urea cycle by excreting hippurate and phenylacetylglutamine, and hemodialyze for ammonia >500 (or persistent encephalopathy).
